intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users want ingredient lists to feel effortless and progressive—each item animates onto the screen rather than loading instantly—reducing cognitive load and making cooking feel less overwhelming
facade.axisTargeted: animated progressive reveal vs instant static dump
facade.label: Ingredients slide in
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use staggered entrance animations, smooth scroll-reveals, and gesture-driven ingredient discovery rather than info-dumps
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer speed and directness—instant full lists, no animation overhead, fast visual scanning without motion distraction

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to irreverent, witty copy that validates their frustration rather than cheerful, motivational tone
facade.axisTargeted: Voice personality — from earnest/motivational to irreverent/sarcastic
facade.label: Sarcastic and sassy
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into humor that mocks cooking culture and celebrates shortcuts; use casual profanity or bold negativity about traditional cooking
facade.rejectImplies: Stay warm and encouraging; position the app as a helpful friend, not a comedian; avoid poking fun at users' reluctance to cook

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer gentle, approachable visual environments that feel forgiving and low-pressure rather than stark or clinical interfaces
facade.axisTargeted: Color temperature and saturation (warm/soft vs cool/stark)
facade.label: Soft pastel backgrounds
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use muted, warm color palettes to create a sense of ease and reduce cooking anxiety
facade.rejectImplies: Users want either brighter, more energetic visuals or prefer minimal/monochromatic design that feels honest rather than cozy

evidence.hypothesis: Users want ingredient lists to feel effortless and progressive—each item animates onto the screen rather than loading instantly—reducing cognitive load and making cooking feel less overwhelming
evidence.implication: The app should use staggered entrance animations, smooth scroll-reveals, and gesture-driven ingredient discovery rather than info-dumps

facade.hypothesis: For people who hate cooking, friction reduction (minimal steps, auto-filled inputs) matters more than culinary depth. This tests whether the mood skews toward UTILITARIAN EFFICIENCY or PLAYFUL DELIGHT.
facade.label: One-tap meals
facade.acceptImplies: User values speed and friction-removal over entertainment. Lean toward streamlined, no-nonsense interface. Consider auto-fill, one-button ordering, progress bars, neutral grays and whites.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants cooking to feel less like a chore and more like something fun or social. Lean toward encouragement, humor, community feel, warmer tones, celebration of small wins.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to seeing just the ingredients they need, stripped of all narrative, history, or cooking explanations
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data vs key metrics — specifically, ingredient-focused minimalism vs narrative-rich recipe context
facade.label: Ingredient list only
facade.acceptImplies: The app should prioritize ingredient lists as the primary information hierarchy, potentially showing recipes as ingredient-first, with cooking steps secondary or on-demand
facade.rejectImplies: Users need more context than just ingredients — they want narrative scaffolding, warnings, substitutions, or story to feel confident attempting the recipe

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking prefer seeing all recipes at once in a single continuous scroll, reducing cognitive load from navigation decisions.
facade.label: One long scrolling list
facade.acceptImplies: User values minimal clicks and decision points; design should flatten hierarchy and use infinite/long scroll for recipe discovery.
facade.rejectImplies: User needs structure and breaks; consider pagination, tabs, or sections to chunk the recipe exploration experience.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user needs heavy filtering (dietary, time, skill) upfront vs. minimal friction and discovery-based browsing
Whether user wants smooth, animated guidance through recipes vs. snappy, instant feedback and quick-tap interactions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user needs heavy filtering (dietary, time, skill) upfront vs. minimal friction and discovery-based browsing
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants smooth, animated guidance through recipes vs. snappy, instant feedback and quick-tap interactions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want immediate feedback and control—tapping ingredients off a list gives them a satisfying sense of progress and completion, reducing the intimidation of a long task by breaking it into micro-wins rather than passive scrolling through static lists
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — gesture-driven interaction (tap-to-remove) vs passive consumption (reading)
facade.label: Ingredients vanish on tap
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use interactive ingredient checking with satisfying disappear animations, haptic feedback, and progress indicators to gamify the cooking prep phase
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer a read-only ingredient list format; interaction should stay minimal and focused on navigation rather than ingredient-level feedback loops

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers only the easiest, fastest meals with minimal ingredients and steps. Perfect for busy people who want to eat well without the cooking fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so easy, you'll actually cook them

Garlic Butter Pasta
10 min 4 ingredients
Boil pasta, toss with butter, garlic, and salt. Done.
Sheet Pan Chicken
25 min 5 ingredients
Throw chicken and veggies on a pan. Roast. Eat.
5-Minute Egg Fried Rice
5 min 4 ingredients
Leftover rice, scrambled egg, soy sauce, heat. Boom.
